Research

2025 Canadian Cyber Risk Report

A look at the threats hitting Canadian organizations hardest this year: ransomware, supply chain exposure, and the compliance pressures bearing down on regulated industries.

May 31, 2025

The Threat Landscape Facing Canadian Organizations in 2025

Canadian organizations faced a materially more complex threat environment in 2025. Ransomware groups continued to prioritize mid-market targets — organizations large enough to pay meaningful ransoms but without the security programs of enterprise firms. Supply chain attacks expanded their footprint, and compliance pressure from regulators intensified across financial services, healthcare, and critical infrastructure.

This report summarizes the key findings from Forecight’s advisory practice over the past year.

Ransomware Remains the Dominant Threat

Ransomware incidents affecting Canadian organizations increased year-over-year, with particularly high concentrations in:

  • Professional services — law firms, accounting firms, and consultancies remain high-value targets due to the sensitivity of client data and historically under-invested security programs
  • Healthcare — patient data commands high prices on criminal markets; operational disruption creates negotiating pressure
  • Manufacturing and logistics — operational downtime creates immediate financial pressure that accelerates ransom payment decisions

The most effective initial access vectors observed were compromised credentials (especially from credential-stuffing attacks against VPNs and remote access tools), phishing, and exploitation of unpatched perimeter systems.

What Works

Organizations that contained ransomware incidents quickly shared a few consistent characteristics: functioning EDR with alert response (not just logging), network segmentation that limited lateral movement, and immutable offsite backups that were regularly tested.

The backup point is worth emphasizing. In incident after incident, the difference between a multi-week outage and a 72-hour recovery was whether backups were genuinely restorable — not just present.

Supply Chain Risk Is No Longer Theoretical

The software supply chain threat that dominated security conversations in 2021–2022 has matured into a persistent operational reality. Third-party software and service dependencies represent meaningful attack surface that most organizations have not fully inventoried, let alone assessed.

Forecight recommends a tiered approach:

  1. Inventory all software with privileged access to your environment — including SaaS platforms with data access
  2. Assess your top 20 vendors against a simple security questionnaire annually
  3. Monitor for breach notifications affecting vendors in your stack
  4. Plan for the failure of critical third-party dependencies

Compliance Pressure Is Increasing

PIPEDA amendments, sector-specific guidance from OSFI and PHIPA regulators, and increasing board-level scrutiny have all raised the stakes for compliance programs. The organizations best positioned are those that have moved compliance from a periodic exercise to an ongoing practice — with documented controls, regular evidence collection, and executive visibility into gaps.

This report reflects observations from Forecight’s advisory practice. For a tailored assessment of your organization’s risk posture, contact our team.
